rookie.title: Silent Partners
rookie.setting: Blue Moon Speakeasy В· Chicago В· 1927
rookie.flavor: The jazz has stopped and the gin has gone cold in the Blue Moon Speakeasy. Behind the velvet curtains and cigarette smoke, someone's final performance has come to an end.
rookie.emptyState: The music died with more than just the night.

items.name: Vincent 'Vinny' Torrino
items.detail: Trumpet player whose solos have been getting shorter lately.

items.name: Stella Marchand
items.detail: Singer whose voice could make angels weep or devils dance.

items.name: Mickey Brennan
items.detail: Bartender who knows everyone's secrets and their poison of choice.

items.name: Dorothy 'Dot' Fleming
items.detail: Piano player with fingers that dance across keys like lightning.

items.name: Poisoned Gin
items.detail: A shot glass still sitting on the bar, crystal clear death.

items.name: Lead Pipe
items.detail: Heavy plumbing from the basement, stained with something dark.

items.name: Broken Bottle
items.detail: Champagne bottle shattered into jagged pieces near the stage.

items.name: Piano Wire
items.detail: Thin steel string that once made music, now silent and deadly.

items.name: Main Bar
items.detail: Where the poison flows freely and last calls echo forever.

items.name: Back Office
items.detail: Private room where the real business of the night gets done.

items.name: Stage Area
items.detail: Under the spotlight where performers live and die by applause.

items.name: Storage Cellar
items.detail: Dark basement where bottles rest and bodies could disappear.

clues.heading: The Final Act
clues.text: Jazz pianist Tommy Castellano lies crumpled behind his piano, a half-empty gin glass nearby. The ivory keys are stained with blood from his final, discordant chord.
clues.alone: A musician's last performance ended in violence and gin. The stage area is the confirmed murder scene.

clues.heading: Backstage Whispers
clues.text: The cleaning lady swears she saw Stella Marchand arguing with someone in the back office around midnight. Stella's red lipstick was smudged and her hands were shaking when she emerged.
clues.alone: A singer's composure cracked behind closed doors.

clues.heading: Deadly Strings
clues.text: A piano wire has been cut from Tommy's instrument, leaving a sharp end that matches wounds on his neck. The wire cutter was found in Mickey Brennan's bartending kit.
clues.alone: Musical instruments can become weapons in skilled hands.

clues.heading: The Final Act
clues.text: Dot Fleming's fingerprints are all over the gin glass, but she claims she was only trying to clean up after finding Tommy's body.
clues.alone: Clean hands don't always mean a clean conscience.

clues.heading: Backstage Whispers
clues.text: Stella produces a love letter from Vincent 'Vinny' Torrino, proving their secret romance and confirming he was with her during the murder.
clues.alone: Love letters can be the strongest alibi of all.

clues.heading: Deadly Strings
clues.text: Mickey Brennan's tools were stolen from behind the bar earlier that evening, witnessed by three patrons who saw Dot Fleming near his station.
clues.alone: Sometimes the guilty borrow tools from the innocent. Mickey Brennan is cleared with multiple witnesses.

rookie.epilogue: Dot Fleming had grown tired of living in Tommy Castellano's shadow, watching him take credit for her musical arrangements night after night. When Tommy threatened to fire her for demanding recognition, she snapped, using her intimate knowledge of the piano to turn his own instrument against him. The stolen piano wire became her weapon of choice, and the stage where she'd been overlooked became the scene of her final, deadly performance. As the police led her away, her fingers still moved as if playing invisible keys.

clues.heading: The Final Act
clues.text: Jazz pianist Tommy Castellano lies crumpled behind his piano, a half-empty gin glass nearby. The ivory keys are stained with blood from his final, discordant chord. Vincent 'Vinny' Torrino was.
clues.alone: A musician's last performance ended in violence and gin. The stage area is confirmed as the murder location.

clues.heading: Reliable Source
clues.text: Police Captain Murphy, who frequents the Blue Moon, swears he saw Frank 'The Fingers' Moretto leaving through the back exit around the time of murder.
clues.alone: A trusted lawman's testimony carries weight in any investigation.

clues.heading: Deadly Strings
clues.text: A piano wire has been cut from Tommy's instrument, leaving a sharp end that matches wounds on his neck.
clues.alone: Musical genius often hides behind false signatures.

clues.heading: The Final Act
clues.text: Dot Fleming's fingerprints are all over the scene, but she claims she was only trying to clean up after finding Tommy's body.
clues.alone: Clean hands don't always mean a clean conscience. Professional jealousy is Dot Fleming's established motive.

clues.heading: Reliable Source
clues.text: Frank Moretto produces gambling receipts from a back-room poker game that ran until 3 AM, with multiple witnesses confirming his presence.
clues.alone: Even the most reliable eyes can deceive in shadow and smoke.

clues.heading: Deadly Strings
clues.text: The handwriting on the sheet music matches Dot Fleming's personal notes found in her dressing room.
clues.alone: Theft of artistic genius cuts deeper than any blade.

detective.epilogue: Dot Fleming had endured months of watching Tommy Castellano steal her musical arrangements and claim them as his own brilliant compositions. When she finally confronted him that night, demanding recognition for her work, Tommy laughed and threatened to fire her if she made trouble. Her years of professional jealousy boiled over into rage, and she used the very piano wire from the instrument that had made him famous to end his thieving career forever. The stage where he'd stolen her spotlight became the scene of her deadly revenge, played out with the precision of a master musician.

items.detail: Trumpet player whose loyalty runs deeper than music.

items.detail: Singer who harmonizes in more than just melody.

items.name: Anthony 'Big Tony' Scalise
items.detail: Businessman whose empire is built on mutual assistance.

items.name: Luca 'Lucky' Benedetto
items.detail: Numbers runner who keeps books and settles scores.

clues.heading: The Final Act
clues.text: Jazz pianist Tommy Castellano lies crumpled behind his piano, a half-empty gin glass nearby. The ivory keys are stained with blood from his final, discordant chord.
clues.alone: A musician's last performance ended in violence and gin. The stage area is the murder location.

clues.heading: Credible Testimony
clues.text: Police Captain Murphy, who frequents the Blue Moon, swears he saw Big Tony Scalise's distinctive silhouette leaving through the back exit around the time of murder.
clues.alone: A veteran cop's eyes rarely deceive him about familiar faces.

clues.heading: Deadly Strings
clues.text: A piano wire has been cut from Tommy's instrument, the sharp end matching wounds on his neck.
clues.alone: Stolen artistry leaves traces in more than just wounded pride.

clues.heading: The Final Act
clues.text: Dot Fleming's fingerprints cover the scene, but multiple witnesses place her performing on stage during the supposed time of death. Her professional jealousy over losing the headline slot drove her.
clues.alone: Professional jealousy is Fleming's established motive.

clues.heading: Credible Testimony
clues.text: Big Tony produces receipts from a high-stakes card game that ran until dawn, with Chicago's elite vouching for his presence.
clues.alone: Shadows and smoke can transform any silhouette into a familiar enemy.

clues.heading: Deadly Strings
clues.text: Luca 'Lucky' Benedetto's business card falls from Tommy's pocket, with a time written on it matching exactly when the murder occurred.
clues.alone: Numbers runners keep precise schedules, especially for deadly appointments.

inspector.epilogue: Dot Fleming had suffered in silence for months as Tommy Castellano stole her musical compositions and claimed them as his own genius. When confrontation failed, she turned to Luca 'Lucky' Benedetto, who owed her a favor from their childhood in Little Italy. Lucky created the perfect alibi by ensuring she was performing on stage while he lured Tommy backstage with promises of a business opportunity. Together, they orchestrated Tommy's murder with the precision of a musical score, using the very piano wire from the instrument that had made him famous. Professional jealousy had found its deadly harmony through the bonds of old neighborhood loyalty.
